solution:

⇒ηY3k=3η-Y⇒k=ηY9η-3Y N m-2.

options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct
solution:
The relationship between Young's modulus, stress, and strain is:

stressstrain=Y

Since the elastic modulus of steel is more, for the same amount of stress, the strain will be less resulting in less deformation of buildings and bridges. Therefore, steel is used for construction of large structures. 

Hence, both assertion and reason are correct and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{gh}}{\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}\right)} \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}} \times 100=\frac{\rho \mathrm{gh}}{\mathrm{B}} \times 100 \\ & \frac{1000 \times 10 \times 2.5 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^9} \times 100 \% \\ & =1.25 \%\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Bulk modulus is given by $B = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}$

The fractional decrease in volume is $-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{B}$

Substituting the given values:

$-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{6.3 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^9} = 3 \times 10^{-2}$

The percentage decrease in volume is $\left(-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \times 100$

Percentage decrease $= 3 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 = 3\%$

Answer: $3\%$

solution:

⇒10F2AY+10=11F1AY+11

Substituting F1=100 N, F2=120 N

⇒1200AY−1100AY=1⇒100AY=1

⇒1AY=1100

Using equation (i),

 L=l1F1AY+1=l1100100+1=l12

subject: Physics
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}=2$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}$
Given, $F_A=F_B$ and $Y_A=Y_B$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{~d}_{\mathrm{B}}^2}{\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{~d}_{\mathrm{A}}^2}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)^2$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{12}$

solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}_1 \ell_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \ell_2 \Rightarrow \ell_2=\frac{\mathrm{A}_1 \ell_1}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{A} \times \ell_1}{3 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\ell_1}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=3 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{x}_1=\frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~A} \gamma} \times \ell_1 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{x}_2=\frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{3 \mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_2 \\
& \text { Here } \Delta \mathrm{x}_1=\Delta \mathrm{x}_2 \\
& \frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{3 \mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_2=\frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_1 \\
& \left.\mathrm{~F}_2=3 \mathrm{~F}_1 \times \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=3 \mathrm{~F}_1 \times 3=\mathrm{i}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given parameters:
$L = 3\text{ m}$
$\Delta L = 3 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}$
$V = 600 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^3$
$Y = 1.1 \times 10^{11}\text{ N/m}^2$

Strain is given by:
$\text{Strain} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 10^{-3}$

Elastic potential energy stored in the wire is:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{Stress} \times \text{Strain} \times \text{Volume}$

Since $\text{Stress} = Y \times \text{Strain}$:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times Y \times (\text{Strain})^2 \times V$

Substituting the values:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (1.1 \times 10^{11}) \times (10^{-3})^2 \times (600 \times 10^{-6})$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.1 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-6} \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.1 \times 600 \times 10^{-1}$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 66 = 33\text{ J}$

Answer: $33$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given that the wires have equal cross-sectional area $A$ and are under the same tension $F$, the elongation $\Delta L$ is given by:

$\Delta L = \dfrac{F L}{A Y}$

Since the elongations in both wires are equal ($\Delta L_A = \Delta L_B$):

$\dfrac{F L_A}{A Y_A} = \dfrac{F L_B}{A Y_B}$

$\dfrac{L_A}{Y_A} = \dfrac{L_B}{Y_B}$

$L_B = L_A \times \dfrac{Y_B}{Y_A}$

Substituting the given values $L_A = 2.2$ m and $\dfrac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \dfrac{20}{11}$:

$L_B = 2.2 \times \dfrac{11}{20}$

$L_B = \dfrac{24.2}{20} = 1.21$ m

Answer: $1.21$

solution: In vertical direction $2 \mathrm{~T} \sin \theta=20$ using small angle approximation $\sin \theta=\theta$ $\begin{aligned} & \theta=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}=\frac{10}{\theta} \\ & \mathrm{T}=1000 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{array}{ll} \text {Change in length } \Delta \mathrm{L} =2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{L}^2}-2 \mathrm{~L} \\ =2 \mathrm{~L}\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{2 \mathrm{~L}^2}-1\right] \\ \Delta \mathrm{L} =\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{~L}} \end{array}$ $\therefore$ Modulus of elasticity $=\frac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }}$ $\begin{aligned} & 2 \times 10^{11}=\frac{10^3}{\mathrm{~A} \times \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{~L}}} \times 2 \mathrm{~L} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{A}=1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2 \end{aligned}$

The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material is given by

Y=stressstrain=stress∆lL   ...1

The formula to calculate the potential energy U stored into the wire is given by

U=12×stress×strain×volume=12×stress×∆lL×A×L= 12×stress×A×∆l   ...2

From equation (1) and equation (2), it can be written that

U=12×Y×∆l2L×A   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the required cross-sectional area of the wire.

80 J=12×2.0×1011 Nm-2×0.02 m220 m×A⇒A= 2×80 J×202.0×1011×4×10-4 Nm-1=4×10-5 m2×106 mm21 m2= 40 mm2
